title: Results at a glance: Evaluation of the Corporate Data and Surveillance Modernization Activities: Public Health Agency of Canada - Canada.ca
content:
## Results at a glance: Evaluation of the Corporate Data and Surveillance Modernization Activities: Public Health Agency of Canada
October 2024 Office of  Audit and Evaluation

## Context and activities
In 2020, the Public Health  Agency of Canada (PHAC) launched the Data, Surveillance and Foresight Branch  (DSFB) to address longstanding issues with Canada’s public health data system.  Over the past four years, DSFB coordinated the Agency’s efforts to improve  public health data and surveillance through a range of modernization activities  done in collaboration with internal and external health data partners. Key commitments  include:
- Development of the Federal, Provincial, and  Territorial (FPT) Health Data Commitments and the Joint  FPT Action Plan on Health Data and Digital Health , following  recommendations from the Pan-Canadian Health Data Strategy
- Implementation of DSFB-led data and surveillance  modernization activities funded within the Detect-Understand-Act (DUA)  Framework
- Implementation and renewal of the 2019 PHAC Data  Strategy
The evaluation examined the progress, achievements, and  approach that PHAC has made from January 2020 to August 2024 on commitments identified  above. The evaluation developed findings based on a triangulation of evidence  from various sources including a review of internal documents and performance  data, internal and external interviews, an international comparative analysis,  case studies, and a scoping literature review.

### What the evaluation found
- Over the past four years, PHAC has implemented  new and innovative data and surveillance modernization initiatives to improve  public health data sharing, collection, and use within PHAC and with its  partners. Key achievements within PHAC included the development of an integrated  risk assessment function, improved horizontal surveillance coordination, and  data infrastructure improvements. In addition, PHAC, in partnership with Health  Canada and PT governments, developed the FPT Health Data Commitments and Joint  Action Plan to advance intergovernmental collaboration on health data. PHAC advanced  these commitments despite pandemic interruptions, limitations in a federated  health system, and administrative delays.
- PHAC leveraged significant but temporary funding  related to the pandemic response to address longstanding and widely  acknowledged needs to improve the pan-Canadian public health data system.  Despite many beneficial outputs and progress both to improve PHAC’s internal  functions and support external partnerships, work remains to address ongoing  barriers to the collection, sharing, access, and use of health data for public  health purposes in Canada. Meanwhile, since the COVID-19 pandemic, there has  been eroding public trust in public health and government institutions affecting  health data sharing and use. Finally, given the changing context of public  health, partners and experts are increasingly emphasizing non-technological  health data priorities, including improving health equity data and enhancing  Indigenous engagement and data sovereignty.
- While creating a dedicated branch for corporate  data and surveillance filled gaps in capacity and mandate, there remain  opportunities for PHAC to improve uptake of corporate modernization initiatives  by further clarifying and communicating roles and responsibilities, identifying  a common set of public health-driven priorities and vision to connect  initiatives, and improving internal coordination.
- Coordinate  PHAC’s ongoing and planned corporate data and surveillance modernization  initiatives, and ensure they all align with a common, clearly defined, and  public health-driven vision.
- Clarify  and communicate roles and responsibilities at PHAC for public health data and  surveillance to Agency staff and external data partners.
- Develop  a feasible and realistic implementation plan to achieving the shared vision  defined in Recommendation 1, based on the accountabilities defined in  Recommendation 2.
